finding: Decoded GitHub OIDC claims must not reach authorization policy until the compact JWT signature has been verified against a pinned GitHub issuer discovery contract and one unambiguous RS256 JWKS key.

primary_sources.supports: GitHub issues a job-unique JWT with RS256 header and workflow identity claims for provider validation.
GitHub Actions OIDC issuer and claim semantics.
JWS compact signing input and signature validation semantics.
JWK and JWKS key representation and key identifiers.
Issuer metadata and jwks_uri discovery semantics.

prior_work_compared: R-005 immutable OIDC subject identity policy
design_implication: Expose one ordered entrypoint that returns claims only after signature verification succeeds, then applies R-006 authorization. Signature failure must prevent authorization evaluation; authorization failure must suppress claims from the accepted result.
evidence_strength: committed implementation and adversarial tests; functionally equivalent cryptographic core executed locally, exact committed composed chain not yet executed
uncertainty: Network retrieval, TLS validation, JWKS cache lifetime, key rotation, replay storage, and live GitHub token verification are outside this artifact.
falsification_route: Run node --test tools/frontier-research/github-oidc-verification-chain.test.mjs; then test a real GitHub-issued token with retained discovery and JWKS bytes. Any acceptance of a tampered token, wrong workflow SHA, attacker-directed key URI, ambiguous kid, stale token, or mismatched audience falsifies the contract.
